title: 🎙️ Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life
description: Today we head into the wild — the Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) plateau, river valleys, and backcountry that define the Cariboo experience. We're covering the technology helping monitor, protect, and navigate these landscapes: wildfire detection, wildlife tracking, satellite communication, and the tools keeping backcountry travellers safe and conservationists informed. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we head into the wild — the Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) plateau, river valleys, and backcountry that define the Cariboo experience. We're covering the technology helping monitor, protect, and navigate these landscapes: wildfire detection, wildlife tracking, satellite communication, and the tools keeping backcountry travellers safe and conservationists informed.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life' through a RURAL TECH AND INNOVATION lens. Strong fits include: wildfire detection tech and BC Wildfire monitoring systems, satellite/drone surveillance for Chilcotin wilderness, wildlife tracking and conservation technology, watershed sensors and salmon habitat monitoring, weather stations and climate tracking in backcountry, avalanche forecasting tech, trail mapping and GPS systems, outdoor recreation apps and connectivity, backcountry communication devices (satellite messengers, Meshtastic), flood/drought prediction systems, remote cameras for wildlife research, and how technology helps manage and protect Cariboo wild spaces. Prefer articles that bridge wilderness/conservation AND technology. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): prehistoric and ancient biology (fossils, palaeontology, deep-time creatures) with no contemporary conservation technology angle — these belong to Sunday's Science theme; AI and data centre infrastructure stories about business strategy, investment, or corporate politics rather than direct wilderness, ecosystem, or land-use impact in BC; geopolitical conflict, war, and crime news with no outdoor or conservation relevance; consumer tech reviews and financial market stories; general political and judicial news. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Harvard scientists turn a silicon chip into a DNA writing machine
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Scientists have created a silicon chip that can write dozens of DNA sequences simultaneously using electricity and water-based enzymes, offering a cleaner alternative to conventional DNA manufacturing. The breakthrough could eventually support portable DNA-writing devices and even massive DNA data storage, although new chemistry will be needed to scale the technology further.

The breakthrough could eventually support portable DNA-writing devices and even massive DNA data

items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

A cybersecurity startup dangling millions of dollars to acquire zero-day security vulnerabilities in popular software is run by a pair of far-right conspiracy theorists and convicted felons whose most recent ventures included fake intelligence companies and a now-defunct AI-based lobbying platform they operated under assumed names.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Scientists working to uncover space-based nuclear weapons
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

In a perfect world, the 1967 Outer Space Treaty would be honoured by every nuclear-armed nation in the world, allowing everyone to breath easy about the fact that, of all the ways we humans have sorted out how to kill one another, at least we can rule out atomic fire from beyond our planet's atmosphere (yeah, ballistic missiles get high up there, but work with me here). — Read the rest

The post Scientists working to uncover space-based nuclear weapons appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Rare goblin shark filmed alive for the first time in the deep sea
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

For the first time, researchers have filmed the elusive goblin shark alive in the deep ocean where it naturally lives. The remarkable sightings greatly expand the shark's known range and depth, showing that this 125-million-year-old "living fossil" still has plenty of secrets left to reveal.
items.summary: For the first time, researchers have filmed the elusive goblin shark alive in the deep ocean where it naturally lives. The remarkable sightings greatly expand the shark's known range and depth, showing that this 125-million-year-old "living fossil" still has plenty of secrets left to reveal.

items.title: [Boing Boing] We might be getting that New Vegas followup, but not in the way anyone expected
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

As has been discussed to death both here and on the Internet at large, Fallout: New Vegas is one of the best RPGs ever made. Although it started life as a contracted-out amuse-bouche to keep Fallout fans happy while Bethesda was all hands on deck with Skyrim, Obsidian — an RPG studio formed from the same ex-Black Isle devs who created the series to begin with — knocked it out of the park nonetheless. — Read the rest

The post We might be getting that New Vegas followup, but not in the way anyone expected appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] The Ozempic and Wegovy mistake sending thousands to poison control
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Poison control calls involving semaglutide (Ozempic and Wegovy) soared after the drug was approved for weight management, with researchers linking the increase to accidental dosing mistakes rather than intentional misuse. Simple education about proper weekly dosing and gradual dose increases could help prevent many of these avoidable incidents.

items.title: [New Atlas] Morphing, color-changing liquid stores energy by “charging” into a gel
items.content_html:
🌲 ✏️

Energy storage usually brings to mind batteries, capacitors, tanks of hydrogen, or maybe some giant gravity system hauling blocks up a tower. Northwestern University researchers have now demonstrated something a lot stranger: a yellow liquid that “charges” by rebuilding itself into a black gel, stores electrons for months, then releases them on demand to drive chemical reactions.

Continue Reading

Category: Energy, Engineering

Tags: Electricity, Northwestern University, Materials
